# What Git Is and Why Researchers Need It

Open the folder where your last paper lived. If it looks anything
like most researchers' folders, you will find a small graveyard:

```
paper.docx
paper_v2.docx
paper_v2_JM_edits.docx
paper_FINAL.docx
paper_FINAL_v2.docx
paper_FINAL_actually_this_one.docx
analysis_old/
analysis_new/
analysis_new_DONT_DELETE/
```

Every name in that list is a human being trying to do version
control by hand, and failing in a predictable way. You cannot tell
which file made the table in the submitted PDF. You cannot tell what
changed between `v2` and `FINAL`. If your coauthor edited
`paper_FINAL.docx` while you edited `paper_FINAL_v2.docx`, merging
the two is an evening of copy-pasting and praying. This chapter is
about the tool that makes the graveyard unnecessary. It does not
assume you have installed anything yet; installation is Chapter 4.
The aim here is only to convince you the tool is worth installing.

## What Git actually is

Git is a **version control system**: a program that records the full
history of a project as a series of snapshots, so you can see what
changed, when, and why, and return to any earlier state on demand. A
single saved snapshot is called a **commit**. Instead of a folder
full of `_FINAL_v2` files, you keep one folder and let Git remember
every version of it for you.

The whole history lives in a hidden `.git` subfolder inside your
project. That collection of snapshots is called a **repository**, or
repo. Crucially, it is all on your own machine: Git was built to work
with no network at all. GitHub, the website most people associate
with Git, is a separate thing, a place to put a copy of your repo so
others can see it. You can do months of real, version-controlled
research before you ever touch GitHub, and this book deliberately
keeps it out of the picture until Chapter 9.

Here is what the history of a real project looks like. This is the
running example used throughout the book, a simulated minimum-wage
study, viewed with one command. You will learn to produce this
yourself in Chapter 5; for now just read it.

```bash
$ git log --oneline
dcb18de Note headline result in README
f9a193f Add DiD analysis, report, and main table
758175e Add polars cleaning script
20c7835 Set up project skeleton and data generator
```

Four snapshots, newest on top, each with a short fingerprint (the
**hash**, like `dcb18de`) and a one-line message saying what that
snapshot accomplished. That is the entire project's life story in
four lines: skeleton, cleaning, analysis, write-up. Nobody had to
name a file `_FINAL`. The history is the record.

And at any moment you can ask Git where things stand:

```bash
$ git status
On branch main
nothing to commit, working tree clean
```

"Working tree clean" means every change you have made is already
saved into a commit. That single line is the calm you are buying:
proof that nothing is unsaved and nothing is lost.

## What versioning actually buys a researcher

The `_FINAL_v2` habit is not just ugly. It fails at the specific
things empirical research needs. Git succeeds at each one.

**A trustworthy record of what produced a result.** When a referee
asks "which version of the code made Table 3?", a folder of dated
files cannot answer honestly. A Git history can: the commit that
added the table is right there, and you can return the whole project
to that exact state and rerun it. The link between a result and the
code that produced it stops being a matter of memory.

**Fearless experimentation.** The reason people hoard
`analysis_new_DONT_DELETE/` folders is fear: if I change this, can I
get the old version back? With Git the answer is always yes, so you
stop hoarding. You try the alternative specification, and if it is
worse you discard it in one command and the previous state returns
untouched. Chapter 7 shows how **branches** let you run a whole
speculative line of work in parallel and either keep it or throw it
away.

**Real collaboration without overwrites.** When two coauthors edit a
shared Dropbox file at the same time, one person's work silently
wins and the other's is gone, or Dropbox saves a baffling "conflicted
copy." Git was designed for exactly this case: it merges independent
changes automatically and, when two people truly changed the same
line, it stops and asks you to choose rather than picking for you.
Chapters 9 to 12 build this up.

**An honest message attached to every change.** Each commit carries
a message, so the history explains itself: not just that the data
file changed, but that you "dropped counties with fewer than 100
teens." Six months later that log is often more useful than your
own memory of what you were thinking.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
A committed result is tied to the exact state of every script that
produced it. If your `git log` says the main table came from commit
`f9a193f`, you can return the project to `f9a193f` and regenerate it.
That is the difference between "the figure in the paper" and "a
figure I can reproduce from a known, labelled state of the code." A
reproducible project is one where the history, not your memory, is
the source of truth.
:::

## Git versus the things you are using instead

Most researchers already use *something* to manage versions and
share files. It helps to be precise about what those tools do and do
not do, compared with Git.

| Tool | Keeps full history? | Merges concurrent edits? | Records why a change was made? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Renaming files (`_v2`, `_FINAL`) | Only what you manually kept | No | Only in the filename |
| Dropbox / Google Drive | Limited, time-based versions | No (last save wins, or "conflicted copy") | No |
| Emailing files around | Scattered across inboxes | No | In the email body, if at all |
| Track Changes in Word | Within one document only | One reviewer at a time | Per edit, but not project-wide |
| Git | Yes, every commit, forever | Yes, line by line | Yes, a message per commit |

Dropbox and Google Drive are genuinely useful, and they do keep some
version history. But their history is time-based and shallow ("the
file as it was last Tuesday"), not meaning-based ("the version where
I switched to clustered standard errors"), and they have no concept
of merging two people's simultaneous code edits. They are good at
syncing files. They are not version control for a code-driven
analysis.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
A tempting non-solution is to put your project folder inside Dropbox
and also use Git inside it. This usually backfires: Dropbox and Git
both try to manage the same hidden `.git` folder, and Dropbox can
sync it mid-write and corrupt the repository. Pick one tool to own
the project. If you want both a Git history and cloud backup, the
right pattern is Git plus a remote like GitHub (Chapter 9), not Git
nested inside a file-syncing folder.
:::

## What Git does not do

Git earns trust partly by being honest about its limits. It is a
versioning tool for **text**: source code, scripts, LaTeX, Markdown,
small configuration files. Several things researchers care about fall
outside that, and pretending otherwise is how repositories get
ruined.

**Git does not version large data well.** Git was built for text it
can compare line by line. A multi-megabyte CSV or a binary `.dta`
gets stored as an opaque lump, and every revision stores another full
lump, so the repository balloons and never shrinks. GitHub will warn
above 50 MiB and reject files over 100 MiB outright. Data needs a
different home: a regenerating script, Git LFS, or a data repository
with a DOI. Chapter 8 covers the options.

**Git does not reproduce your software environment.** A commit
records your code, not the versions of polars, pyfixest, or R that
ran it. Code that reproduced a number last year can produce a
different number after a library update. Reproducibility needs the
code (Git) *and* a pinned environment (a lockfile such as `uv.lock`
or `renv.lock`). This pairing is the spine of Chapter 8.

**Git does not store secrets safely.** Anything you commit is in the
history, and history is sticky: deleting a file in a later commit
does not remove it from earlier ones. An API key or a password
committed once must be treated as leaked and rotated, even after you
"delete" it. Chapter 6 is entirely about what must never be committed
and how to clean up if it was.

The one-sentence version: **Git versions the text that describes your
research; it does not, by itself, carry your data or your
environment.** Keep that line in mind and most beginner disasters
never happen.

## What you can now do

You can say what Git is (a system that records your project as a
history of commits), name the concrete things versioning buys a
researcher (a trustworthy record, fearless experimentation, real
collaboration, a self-explaining log), and state plainly what Git
does not handle (large data, software environments, secrets). That is
enough motivation to install it. Chapter 3 draws the mental model
that makes the commands feel obvious, and Chapter 4 gets Git running
on your machine.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

No Git required yet; this is a paper exercise.

1. Open the folder of your most recent project or paper. List every
   file or folder whose name encodes a version by hand (`_v2`,
   `_FINAL`, `_old`, a date, initials). For each one, write the
   single commit message it *should* have had.
2. Find one moment in that project where you lost work, overwrote a
   coauthor, or could not tell which file made a result. Write two
   sentences on which capability from this chapter would have
   prevented it.
3. List the three largest files in the project. For each, decide
   whether it is text Git should version or data that belongs
   elsewhere (look ahead to Chapter 8 if unsure). This is the
   judgment that keeps a research repo healthy.
